sentences.text: Yes, not only my father but also my brother, who was also joining me in a cave, and the whole family happened to be together.
After that, it was no more boring or no more cold, and I was happy.
And I heard my father's story of survival.

He told us about that Pusztavam episode, and we talked about the possibility of our future and what we're going to do, and how we're going to get over it, and how lucky we are because all four of us is together.
We still was hoping we have other relatives who survived, but we didn't know anything about anybody.
So we stayed in that cave till the Russians liberated us.
During the last week, before the Russians came in and the Germans was moving out, we even got brave enough in the middle of the night to come up to fresh air.
I remember the first time they brought me up, I faded.
I couldn't take the fresh air.
It took me a long time before I was able to get back to the normal air because I was there the longest.
And I was looking forward to the time we be free again.
And I consider it one of the most funny, irony of life because after liberation, we came up, and we had our first free day in the house of my uncle, whose-- whose home was just above the cellar.
The cellar was part of the house cellar.
Klima.
Antal, A-N-T-A-L.
My brother had to leave the labor camp when the transfer came between Jelsava and Pusztavam.
The head of the labor camp told my father he is no longer feel safe to hide my brother and please try to find some other place for him.
So he came and he stayed with far relatives in different location, and he was outside.
He never looked Jewish, and he just took a chance.
Beside, by that time, there was no Jewish population in the country, and everybody was kind of sure there cannot be nobody, free Jewish.
And seeing a beautiful kid, like my brother was, nobody assumed any more.
The only thing, he had to be careful not to get into a situation when they would ask for his identity.
So as long as he stayed inside the house or just in a backyard, he was safe enough.
So he also came to join us once he knew his family is in that cave.
And he also came down, but he was also a lot of time upstairs.
But I had to wait till the Russians took over the town.
And it was so ironical because the second day, we started to hear stories and screaming of girls and ladies, who was raped by the Russian soldiers.
They were terrible what they were doing.
Nobody was safe.
They got drunk during the night, and they had rampage through the city.
So my uncle decided, back to the cave-- all the womenfolks back to the cave.
Even two neighboring beautiful daughters came with us until that first group of Russians passed through the town before the Russian authorities arrived, who had some control over the army.
We all had to go back to the cave for about two days, two extra days.
And after that, we decided to go back to our village.
We were so anxious to go back and see what happened to our home and to wait, when some other relatives may show up.
So we couldn't wait any longer.
My aunts and uncle gave us extra food to the trip, but we tried not to take too much because it was a long walk.
And I recall that walk back home, there was still a lot of shooting.
The front was not too far away.
Some of those guns' bullet went above our head.
And I was scared, and my father said, no, they are far away.
You just think it's close.
They convinced me they are far, but I know they were not very far away.
We spend the first night with that friend who-- who helped me escape the very first time with the motorcycle.
Because the walk was too long to do it during a day and there was a curfew.
You had to be inside a house before sundown.
We knew we're never going to make it all the way home.
So halfway we stopped, and we stayed overnight to his house.
And then we followed next day, the road and went all the way home.
It was an interesting episode once we reached the town, though we were all very happy.
And we were walking on the beginning of the main street, and there was some peasant womans who passed us.
They greeting us, said hello.
But kind of strange look on their face.
And just when they passed, I noticed they dropped something on the floor, on the ground, and it was one of my doll.
Nobody had a doll like that, not in that town, and I recognized it.
And I looked at my mom.
She looked at me, and she pulled my sleeves to point me, don't say anything, and we did not.
When we got close to the house, we saw it was completely destroyed.
It was not everything was just missing, but I never saw debris at least this high around every home.
There was not one single window, there was not one door on the house.
And all that debris-- it was amazing.
My father went over to a neighbor and borrowed some shovels, and we had to shovel ourselves out through the window.
We threw out all the dirt and everything we find on the floor.
And we decided to concentrate on one room, one of the warmest, inner room, not one of the city, on the street side.
And some neighbors came over to help with his tools, and they boarded up the window, and they made a makeshift door.
And once the room was clean, we got some fresh hay from a neighbor, and we covered the floor with hay, and that's where we slept the first night.
But by the evening, my grandmother came back, who was hiding in the next town.
She was hiding from the Russians.
Yes, and my beautiful aunts, they were-- all had to hide from the-- the soldiers So when they heard that we are back, they came, and we all slept in that one room, eventually, it was 11 of us.
And I remember that first night, nobody cried but laughed our head off.
I never remember so much giggling and laughing.
We thought it was so funny, from all the money that we used to had, and now, we are sleeping on the hay but every one of us together.
It was a beautiful occasion.
We didn't cried about lost things.
We were all healthy and together.
And then little by little, the next day and so, people started to return our thing, voluntary.
They came up and said, well, we took that because we knew if somebody else take it, they may destroy it or take it in a city and sell it.
We kept-- we took it for safekeeping and we are now returning it.
And perhaps some of them did.
Some of them took it because well, they might never come back, and why shouldn't I have it?
So we were very thankful and assured them we are very appreciative.
Some of the people came who didn't had any of our belonging but they came to offer food.
We received a chicken from one of the local peasants.
I remember the chicken died in old age because we refused to kill her.
She gave us an egg every day of her life, and that egg, during a period of three, four years, was more precious and to have a feast on one day.
And everybody gave us a little.
Nobody had a lot because the German army took a lot of things and whatever was left, the Russians did.
There was no house anymore, like cows and pigs and chickens.
Most of them were killed for the Russian army, and everybody was poor.
So if somebody gave us two, three corn, it was a gift.
And we needed all those gifts to start our life.
But we managed.
My father just looked around, and he realized we had to work really hard if we want to get back on our feet.
But the spring approaching real fast, we have to go out to the field and work on-- on the vineyard.
And once we have grapes, we will have wines, and next year, we will have money.
The only main problem we had-- we needed people to work on those fields.
We had quite a few acre of vineyard and that just so much that little family can work on.
And we couldn't hire anybody because there was no cash.
And people don't work on the say-so next year you get paid.
So what we had to do, we had to do what we could all by ourselves, and we worked our butts off.
I remember my hand as a 12, 13 almost-- it was so sore.
It was full of cuts and bruises.
One day, on one weekend, we stayed home, and I wanted to play my piano.
And I couldn't, and I cried.
My hand was so bad, but they cried with me, and they assured me it's not going to be very long.
At the same time, we got-- we were very lucky because our friend, who later became my mother's second husband, he came back-- the one who became a partisan.
And as a partisan he was captured and transferred into Germany, where he was liberated by the English and Americans.
And he was able to come back.
And the first place he looked, to see if we are safe.
I guess he was looking for my mother, to who he was in love for a long time by then.
And before he went into the service, he gave a suggestion to my father, and he decided to take apart our car because the car was the first thing the Hungarian army just confiscated.
They needed vehicles.
And he took apart the car.
I mean, took it apart to bits of pieces, and every local house kept a piece.
One had a tire.
The other had a carburetor.
The other had a-- the wheel.
Or every little piece of that card was taken apart.
So they couldn't confiscate it when they came around.
And my grandmother, who owned the car legally, showed them.
Look it, you want that?
They turned away.
And because of being apart, the Russians didn't take it either.
He came up, and he was a fantastic mechanic, and he knew where the parts are.
And he went to collect every piece of the car, and he put it together.
And he said to my father, I make a deal with you.
I borrow your car.
I am going to use it for other purpose, which make money, and I will pay you a very good monthly fee for it.
And that's how my father finally was able to put everything back into order because he received every month that payment.

And our friend used the car for transferring newspapers from the capital city of Budapest into the suburb because all our railroad stations and railroad were ruined in the war, and there was absolutely no transportation.
So a car was as valuable as-- as gold.
And he made very good money with it, because he was also smuggling in a same time.
He went to the Russian border.
He went to the Romanian border, delivering newspaper.
He picked up articles.
Like Russia had a lot of salt, so he would pick up bags of salt.
And on the way back, he brought back the salt and sold it in Budapest.
Next time he was sent to the Austrian border, he picked up matches.
He picked up sugar or anything which the public needed and he would come back and sell that one.
So in no time, he became a rich man.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, he was also a Laszlo, which is a very popular Hungarian name, Mayer.
And after we were really becoming pretty well off, no more financial trouble, my mother left us.
She divorced my father, and she married this young man, Mayer Laszlo.
And after that, he keep on working with the cars, but by that time, he had a taxi company.
He owed a beautiful service garage in Budapest, and he was one of the wealthiest men in the country.
And for very, very long time that we didn't know, but he was also smuggling people.
The taxis not only did a little taxiing during the day, but they also smuggled people out of the country through the Austrian border.
He had contact, and everybody was paid on the way, including the Russian guards.
And for good amount of money, he took a lot of Jewish people through the border, who was illegally emigrating to Palestine.
And I didn't know that.
I had a big mouth as a kid, and it was always the major concern of my parents, try to keep everything away from me because sooner or later, it is not going to be a secret.
So I didn't know that, just after everything was over.

And sadly, toward the end, one of his chauffeur got caught at the border smuggling somebody, and he was brought back and taken to a very infamous police station, where many, many people got beaten to death.
And he got a-- a message-- my stepfather got a message.
His man is in that police station.
He knew sooner or later they will beat it out of him, who is the head of this organization, and he will be picked up.
So over night, he had to disappear, with my mother and his newborn little daughter, who was only six months old, behind.
She-- my mother didn't wanted to go with him.
She couldn't leave my brother and me and everybody behind.
And she said she will follow later.
She will talk us into joining her, and then we all go.
But it never materialized because I would never left my father.
I could never leave my father.
I encouraged my mother to go, and I said, I will understand.
You go, it's your husband, but my brother and I, we go back to father's house and we going to stay with him.
She even promised they will take my father, but I said, I take no chances.
I'm staying with my father.
And after awhile, she just gave up.
And even so, he sent, twice, people to pick up my mom.
One of them was a Swiss diplomat.
I was coming home from school, and my mother said, get your pocket book.
We are leaving.
I said, where?
She said, don't worry.
It's safe.
Somebody will going to take us through the border like a family, who belonged to this diplomat.
And I said, good, you go, but I am going back to my father.
She had a hysterical fit and eventually sent the chauffeur away because couldn't change my mind.
And the same thing happened one more time a couple of months later.
And just after she realized there is no way she can change my mind, she decided she's going to stay.
And because my father still wanted her, she came back to my father.
And eventually they remarried.
My father adopted my sister, and my sister was a practically grown up when she find out my father is not her father.
No, not very long.
His business functioned pretty well till about '48 or the beginning of '49.
Then it was a complete Communist takeover, and they took every businesses away from private individuals.
But because of his expertise and because of his background being in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in labor camps, he was a trusted people in the eyes of the Communists.
And they offered him a very good job to heading a big exporting winery in-- in Miskolc.
And we moved into Miskolc, and that's where he took over a director job in that big winery.
So it wasn't too bad.
It wasn't a big step down.
It was more structured, not what he was used to it, but he adopted very well to this new way of working, And-- So you stayed in Miskolc, and you were married in Miskolc?
Yes, I was.
After a short career in a university, which was cut-- stopped because of my affair with my future husband, who wanted to get married.
And I left the school and came home, and we got married.
And he was a motorcycle racer, which was a very favored sports in Hungary.
And it was very popular.
He was a local hero.
He was something like Mickey Mantle was for the Americans, he was for the Hungarians.
And we lived a very colorful life because of his sports activity.
And I was very happy and still appreciative to be alive and very close to my family.
So when the Hungarian Revolution came, that was the first opportunity for anybody in Hungary to leave the country.
Anybody who had any grievance against the Russian-- the Communist regime, they decided to-- the hell with that.
Let's go.
So did my husband.
He was very anxious to leave Hungary.
He believed he can do much better in the West.
He had a lot of-- he was very self-assured, and he knew he can make it go.
And he wasn't about to stay in that restricted Communist country.
And I said goodbye to him, because I was not about to follow him.
I said, I could never leave my parents.
And if you want to go, I don't want to have it on my conscious I ruined your future and your ambition.
If that is that important to you, I forgive.
I am not going to be angry.
Matter of fact, if you change your mind and you want to come back, I can even promise I will wait for you for a couple years.
Or maybe I will follow you later in a couple of years, but I don't want to hold you back.
Let me stay with my family.
And that's what we did.
We said goodbye to each other one night.
He took off next morning.
I moved back to my parents' house, and I made a new life for myself.
And I think I would have stayed, what a different life I would be today.
But I was keep going to work.
I worked in a travel bureau, and I was very anxious every day to go.
Even so, most places were not open anymore.
There was not even supermarkets operating.
But I liked to go into the office, because that was only place that I got any information about the Revolution, what's going on.
It was at a place where people called in.
They were asking me for transportation, and I was able to find out what's going on in different part of the country.
And that was the only reason I was keep going in.
And one day coming home on a streetcar, came to a very big crossroad, and the streetcar stopped.
And I saw a big commotion on the road, and I looked out.
The window was open, and I heard screaming and yelling.
And it happened to be a gang of people beating an old man.
And I was keep watching, and I realize, I knows that-- I know that man.
He turned out to be a little, old Holocaust survivor.
And I heard ugly anti-Semitic remark.
And this man was bloody, and they were still beating him.
And I got just so angry.
I went home.
I picked up my four-year-old son, and I said, the hell with this country!
I am going.
And that what made up my mind to leave the country.
It was in November, 1956.
